title: Members Show Up. The Schedule Doesn't Take Them Anywhere.
subtitle: Harborline Fitness · studio programming & membership analysis · full-diagnostic mode
why_it_matters: Harborline members book classes at nearly the benchmark rate — then visit 4.1 times a month against Ironworks' 7.6, and route 36% of their bookings through revenue-sharing aggregators. Frequency is retention: closing even part of a 3.5-visit gap is the difference between a member who renews and one who churns at month four.

diagnosis.verdict: A programming problem, not a demand problem.
diagnosis.narrative: Participation is near benchmark — members show up. But they visit half as often as Ironworks members, stay parked in cardio comfort-zone formats, and book through aggregators that take a cut of every class. That pattern says the schedule isn't giving members anywhere to go next: no journeys, no progression, no reason to book through the studio's own app. Acquiring more members would pour new names into the same shallow habit loop.

composition.headline: The buckets that build habit are the ones members don't book
composition.narrative: Booking mix: 44% of Harborline bookings are cardio/conditioning and only 9% strength & progression — the bucket that carries Ironworks' 7.6 visits a month at 38% of bookings. Community programming, the habit glue, is 3% of Harborline bookings against 9%. The formats exist on the schedule; the bookings don't follow, because nothing routes members toward them.

benchmark_note.headline: Pulse proves a colorful schedule isn't a strategy
benchmark_note.narrative: Pulse Collective runs the flashiest specialty menu in the market — aerial, pole, themed rides — and its members still book 47% cardio, visit 4.4 times a month, and leak 41% of bookings to aggregators. Offering exotic formats did not shift behavior. Ironworks shifted behavior with ordinary formats and extraordinary routing: goal-based journeys, next-class suggestions, coach check-ins. The benchmark is Ironworks' member behavior, and the mechanism is guidance, not menu breadth.

channel.headline: 19 points of booking capture, with margin attached
channel.narrative: Harborline's own app and front desk take 52% of bookings; 36% arrive through aggregator platforms that charge a revenue share on every one. Ironworks captures 71% — a 19-point gap. Aggregator members are also the loosest-held: they book single classes, never see a journey, and churn to whatever's nearby. The programs below are the capture play: journeys only exist in the studio's own app.

storefront_critique.headline: A week-grid is a timetable, not a membership
bullets: The app shows a chronological week grid filtered by location — no goal-based grouping, no 'start here' for a new member.
After a booking, nothing suggests the next class; every visit is a cold start.
Strength formats — the highest-affinity, highest-frequency bucket — are visually indistinguishable from everything else on the grid.
The free Community Run Club, the studio's best habit-builder, isn't even listed in the app; it spreads by front-desk word of mouth.

merchandising_benchmark.headline: Ironworks sells journeys; everyone else sells time slots
merchandising_benchmark.narrative: Three named programs, next-step suggestions after every class, coach check-ins at weeks two and six, and an individualized-programming add-on. Pulse markets spectacle and routes nobody. The audit table is the playbook gap in one view.

packs.headline: Three programs, launchable from this week's schedule
packs.narrative: Members over-index on strength training (126) and endurance events (118), and at-index on nutrition (109) — the appetite for progression is already in the base. Every class below is already on the schedule; each program is a routed sequence with a goal, not a new format. Dance under-indexes (84): no dance-led theming, whatever the competitors are doing.

pricing_note.headline: Drop-in prices are at parity; the gap is routing, not rate
pricing_note.narrative: On comparable formats Harborline sits within a couple of dollars of both competitors (Ride 45 $28 vs Beat Ride 45 $28; strength formats $26–28 vs $26–32). Nothing in the price list explains a 3.5-visit frequency gap. One catalog note: the Community Run Club carries no listed price and no app listing — free is a feature, but invisible is not.

now.detail: Routed sequences from existing classes, bookable only in the studio's own app — the capture play and the frequency play are the same feature.
Goal-based groupings above the week grid, a 'start here' path for new members, and a suggested next class after every booking.
Free, in the app, attached to Race Ready — the cheapest habit-builder the studio owns is currently a secret.
Every aggregator visitor gets one in-app offer: first program session free when booked direct.

next.detail: Form check-ins at weeks two and six of Stronger in 12 Weeks — the one new offering the programs need (staff time, not construction).
Quarterly-assessment add-on, following the proven Ironworks model, only after program take-rate proves demand.
A skills/clinic bucket is absent from the schedule entirely; borrow Ironworks' format once strength journeys have an audience.

next_step.headline: One 60-day proof
next_step.narrative: Ship First 30 Days Path to every member who joined in the last 90 days, instrumented: program take-rate, visits per member vs a holdout, and share of those bookings made in the studio's own app. In parallel, pull class-level fill rates by channel — the one dataset this analysis lacked.

mapping_resolutions.reason: unstructured access for members building comfort — an entry-point format, not conditioning programming

comparability_calls: Ride 45 vs Pulse Beat Ride 45 — confirmed comparable ($28 vs $28)
Aerial/pole formats have no Harborline counterpart — excluded from price comparison entirely
IW Progressive Overload Track is an 8-week program priced per class — compared per-session only
data_flags: All figures are synthetic demonstration data (simulated member-app analytics, trailing 90 days); no figure is real market data.
Saturday Community Run Club has no listed price — excluded from price comparison; flagged upstream by validation.
Presentation audit from a simulated app/storefront audit, July 2026.
appendix.figure_types_note: Participation = share of members booking any class in a month (the only share-of-members figure). Booking mix = share of bookings, sums to 100% per studio. Schedule share = share of class formats offered (offer-side, not behavioral). Booking share = share of bookings by channel. Affinity index: 100 = population par. Frequency (median visits per active member per month) is a count, not a share.
